summary: Gruber presents his four favorite Mac apps of 2004: Interarchy 7 (FTP/SFTP client), Affrus 1.0 (Perl debugger), SpamSieve 2.2 (spam filter), and BBEdit 8.0 (text editor). He provides detailed histories and evaluations of each, with particular emphasis on Interarchy's evolution from Anarchie and its superior attention to detail, Affrus's extraordinary documentation, and SpamSieve's near-perfect accuracy enhanced by AppleScript-based score thresholds. Honorable mentions go to NetNewsWire 2.0, Quicksilver, and OmniWeb 5, though the first two are excluded for being technically in beta and OmniWeb lost out due to performance issues compared to Safari.
key_points: Interarchy 7's browser-style interface, tabs, disclosure triangles, and AppleScript support made it the first satisfying file-transfer app for Gruber in nearly a decade
Affrus 1.0's help documentation, written by Matt Neuburg, is singled out as the finest software documentation Gruber has ever encountered, truly leveraging hypertext
SpamSieve 2.2's accuracy reached 99.8%, and using AppleScript to access raw spam scores rather than binary spam/not-spam classification dramatically reduced manual review burden
Public betas like NetNewsWire 2.0 and Quicksilver stretch the concept of beta software to the point where the distinction from release software may not be meaningful
OmniWeb 5 lost to Safari as Gruber's primary browser due to performance and rendering issues, despite superior features like per-site preferences and tab management
money_quotes: What's great about Interarchy isn't that it has tons of features (although it does), but that its features are so well done.
It's the attention to detail that makes Interarchy a pleasure to use.
There are only two types of 1.0 products: those which ship with missing features, and those which never actually ship.
Help is ignored because it tends to utterly suck. (Apple, I am looking in your direction.)
Ignoring Affrus's help would be tragic, because it not only doesn't suck, it is quite simply the finest software documentation I have ever encountered.
SpamSieve epitomizes the idea of utility software that does one thing, and does it well.
I'm not sure how it can be a 'beta' if more people are using it than are using the non-beta 1.0.x releases.
That assumes Quicksilver is ever going to come out of 'beta'.
key_insight: The best software is defined not by feature count but by attention to detail and doing its core job exceptionally well.
